content:
# Leviticus Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67r–67v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_015_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_015_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: **2** "Speak to the people of Israel and tell them: When any man has a discharge from his body, that discharge makes him unclean. **3** This is the condition for his uncleanness from the discharge: whether his body lets the discharge flow, or his body stops up the discharge — it makes him unclean. **4** Every bed the man with the discharge lies on will be unclean, and everything he sits on will be unclean.

**5** "Anyone who touches his bed must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **6** Anyone who sits on something the man with the discharge sat on must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **7** Anyone who touches the body of the man with the discharge must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening.

**8** "If the man with the discharge spits on someone who is clean, that person must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **9** Every saddle the man with the discharge rides on will be unclean. **10** Whoever touches anything that was under him will be unclean until evening, and whoever carries any of those things must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening.

**11** "Anyone the man with the discharge touches without having rinsed his hands in water must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **12** Any clay pot the man with the discharge touches must be smashed, and any wooden vessel must be rinsed with water.

**13** "When the man with the discharge is healed of his discharge, he shall count off seven days for his purification. He shall wash his clothes and bathe his whole body in fresh water, and he will be clean. **14** On the eighth day he shall take two turtledoves or two young pigeons, come before the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting, and give them to the priest. **15** The priest shall offer them — one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering. So the priest shall make atonement before the LORD for the man, on account of his discharge.

**16** "When a man has a seminal emission, he shall bathe his whole body in water and will be unclean until evening. **17** Any clothing or leather on which the emission falls must be washed in water and will be unclean until evening.

**18** "If a man lies with a woman and there is a seminal emission, both of them shall bathe in water and will be unclean until evening.

**19** "When a woman has her regular discharge — her monthly period — the uncleanness of her flow will last seven days. Anyone who touches her will be unclean until evening. **20** Anything she lies on during her period of uncleanness will be unclean, and anything she sits on will be unclean. **21** Anyone who touches her bed must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **22** Anyone who touches anything she sat on must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening. **23** Whether it is something on the bed or something she sat on — when anyone touches it, they will be unclean until evening.

**24** "If a man actually lies with her and her menstrual flow touches him, he will be unclean for seven days, and every bed he lies on will be unclean.

**25** "When a woman has a discharge of blood lasting many days, outside the time of her monthly period — or when her discharge continues beyond her period — all the days of her impure discharge she will be unclean, just as she is during her regular period. **26** Every bed she lies on throughout the duration of her discharge will be treated as if it were her period bed, and anything she sits on will be unclean, just as in the uncleanness of her monthly period. **27** Anyone who touches these things will be unclean; they must wash their clothes, bathe in water, and will be unclean until evening.

**28** "When her discharge clears up, she shall count off seven days, and after that she will be clean. **29** On the eighth day she shall take two turtledoves or two young pigeons and bring them to the priest at the entrance to the tent of meeting. **30** The priest shall offer one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering. So the priest shall make atonement before the LORD for her, on account of her unclean discharge.

**31** "You shall thus keep the people of Israel separate from their impurities, so they do not die in their impurity by defiling my dwelling place that is in their midst."

**32** This is the instruction for the man with a discharge and for the man who has a seminal emission and becomes unclean from it, **33** and for the woman in the uncleanness of her monthly period, and for any man or woman with a discharge, and for the man who lies with an unclean woman.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Body as Theological Territory

Chapter 15 brings the purity section of Leviticus (chapters 11–15) to its completion, and it does so with the most intimate territory of all: the human body's generative and excretory functions. Food (ch.11), childbirth (ch.12), skin and surfaces (chs.13–14) have all been covered; now comes bodily discharge from the reproductive organs. The subject matter is clinical and unavoidable, and Leviticus does not flinch from it.

The controlling idea, stated explicitly in verse 31, is protection of the divine dwelling: "so they do not die in their impurity by defiling my dwelling place that is in their midst." The stakes are not hygiene or social etiquette — they are the continued presence of God in the camp. If Israel's defilements are allowed to accumulate without remedy, the sanctuary becomes contaminated, and a contaminated sanctuary cannot be the site of God's habitation among a mortal people. The entire purity system exists to maintain the conditions under which the holy God can safely dwell with an impure humanity.

This reframes everything. The regulations of chapter 15 are not about shame, prudishness, or treating normal bodily functions as sinful. They are about the management of a very specific kind of incompatibility: the realm of the holy (the sanctuary, the divine presence) and the realm of the fleshly (bodily processes associated with generation, decay, and mortality) cannot mix without catastrophic result. The remedy — washing, waiting, offerings — is practical, accessible, and finite. The uncleanness is temporary. The goal is always restoration.

### Male Abnormal Discharge: The Severity Gradient (vv.1–15)

The chapter opens with what Hebrew calls *zov* — an abnormal bodily discharge, almost certainly a pathological genital emission (gonorrhea and similar conditions have been suggested by commentators). This is distinguished from the normal seminal emission addressed in verses 16–17 by its prolonged and abnormal character.

The contamination here is unusually potent and socially pervasive. The bed, the seat, the saddle, the touched person, the spit — all transmit uncleanness. Even the *objects beneath* the man on his saddle are unclean. Even a clay pot he touches must be destroyed; it cannot be cleansed, only smashed. The escalating scope of contamination — body, possessions, anyone touched, anything touched — conveys that this condition is serious, not something to be managed quietly. The community must know, because the community is affected.

The cure requires counting seven days after the discharge stops (v.13) — a full week of clean days, not merely waiting out a calendar period. Then fresh water bathing and a two-bird offering on the eighth day. The structural parallel to the tzara'at cleansing of chapter 14 is intentional: both require the eighth-day threshold, both require a pair of birds, both require the priest's mediation. The same architecture of restoration applies across different conditions. The God who heals flesh-disease also heals discharge-disease; the path back is the same.

### Seminal Emission: The Lightest Category (vv.16–18)

The sudden shift in verses 16–18 is striking. After the elaborate multi-day contamination regime for *zov*, seminal emission — normal and universal — is handled in two verses: bathe, wait until evening, done. No offering required. No priest required. The uncleanness is real but it is minimal and self-resolving.

This matters theologically. The text is not treating male sexuality as inherently sinful or shameful. The evening deadline on uncleanness means that by the next day's dawn, the person is fully clean. Life resumes. The category exists to protect the sanctuary from casual contamination, not to stigmatize the normal operation of the body. The same applies to intercourse in verse 18: both partners become unclean until evening, then they are clean. A shared ritual bath is implicit; a shared temporary boundary is acknowledged; and then life continues.

### Menstruation: Regular and Prolonged (vv.19–30)

The menstrual law divides into two sharply different situations: normal monthly period (vv.19–24) and abnormal prolonged discharge (vv.25–30).

During the regular period, the woman is unclean for seven days — the duration of a typical cycle. The contamination is potent but bounded. Her bed, her seat, anything she touches transmits uncleanness. Anyone who touches her is unclean until evening (a lighter category). A man who lies with her during her period — even if unknowingly — takes on her seven-day uncleanness status. The text does not say this is sinful; it says it is unclean. Those are different categories in Leviticus's moral architecture.

The prolonged discharge (vv.25–30) is the more serious case. Every day beyond the normal cycle is a day of uncleanness. The bed, the seat, the same contamination web — all of it extends indefinitely as long as the discharge continues. This is not normal bodily function but a condition of ongoing impurity with no self-resolving end. When at last the discharge ceases, she counts seven clean days, then brings two birds for an eighth-day offering.

This is the precise condition of the woman in Mark 5:25–34, who had been hemorrhaging for twelve years. The text there says she had "suffered many things from many physicians," had spent everything she had, and had grown worse. In Leviticus's framework, twelve years of abnormal discharge means twelve years of uncleanness, twelve years of contaminating everything she touched, twelve years of social and ritual exile within her own community. When she pressed through the crowd to touch Jesus's garment — an act that would have made her a contaminating presence among the jostling people around her — she was in every sense a woman who had violated the boundaries of chapter 15. The healing Jesus spoke was not merely physical; it was the declaration that ended twelve years of exclusion: "Daughter, your faith has made you well; go in peace, and be healed of your disease" (Mark 5:34). The priest's declaratory role — "I declare you clean" — finds its ultimate expression there.

### The Purpose Statement: V.31 as the Theological Keystone

Verse 31 is one of the most important verses in the entire purity section. After twenty-eight verses of case law, the text pauses and explains itself: "You shall thus keep the people of Israel separate from their impurities, so they do not die in their impurity by defiling my dwelling place that is in their midst."

Three elements demand attention.

First: *separation from impurities*, not separation from impure people. The grammar is about managing conditions, not excluding persons permanently. The same person who is unclean today can be clean by evening, or in seven days, or on the eighth day. The system is designed for reintegration, not for permanent exclusion.

Second: *lest they die*. The risk is real and the consequence is mortal. A contaminated sanctuary cannot safely house the divine presence; an oblivious people who let impurity pile up without remedy are not merely ritually inconvenienced — they are walking toward a collision between mortal uncleanness and eternal holiness. That collision kills. The purity laws are, in this sense, protective — not of God from the people, but of the people from themselves.

Third: *my dwelling place that is in their midst*. The tabernacle is not a remote shrine on a sacred mountain, safely separated from human contact. It is *in their midst* — embedded in the community, pitched at the center of the camp. This proximity is the gift and the danger. God chose to live close. The consequence of that choice is that the people must manage the interface between the holy and the unholy, the clean and the unclean, with care. Chapter 15 is part of what "care" looks like.

### Symmetry and Theology of the Four Categories

The chapter's structure maps four situations in a deliberate chiasm of severity:

- Male abnormal discharge (*zov*): most severe; seven clean days, eighth-day offerings, priestly mediation (vv.1–15)
- Male seminal emission: lightest; bathe, wait until evening, no offering (vv.16–18)
- Female menstrual period: moderate; seven-day uncleanness, no offering required (vv.19–24)
- Female abnormal discharge: most severe, matching the male *zov*; seven clean days, eighth-day offerings, priestly mediation (vv.25–30)

The symmetry is significant. Male and female, normal and abnormal, brief and prolonged — the law addresses the full range of human bodily experience with proportionate responses. The lightest categories require no priestly involvement and resolve quickly. The most serious require time, counting, and sacrifice. The principle is proportionality: the depth of the ritual response matches the depth of the disruption.

Note also the parallelism between male and female abnormal discharge. Both require identical responses (vv.13–15 and vv.28–30). The woman with prolonged hemorrhage is not in a more severe category than the man with chronic discharge; they are legal and theological equals before the LORD. The text treats them with exact equivalence.

### The Eighth Day Again: Resurrection Arithmetic

As in chapter 14, the eighth day appears here as the moment of re-entry into full covenant standing (vv.14, 29). Seven days of clean waiting — the complete week — and then one day beyond. The number seven in biblical thought marks completion: the seven-day creation week, the seven-year sabbatical cycle, the seven-times-seven of Jubilee. The eighth day is the day after completion — the day of new beginning, of covenant renewal, of the priest's declaratory blessing.

For the person who has endured days, weeks, or years of bodily uncleanness, the eighth day is the day they come back. They bring what they can — two birds, accessible and inexpensive — and the priest makes atonement. The cost is low; the restoration is complete. Whatever had set them apart from the worshiping community is resolved, atoned for, released. They are clean. They may approach.

### LXX Observations

The Septuagint (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 101–102) renders the male abnormal discharge (*zov*) as *rhysis* — "flow" or "flux" — a generic term for bodily discharge. This is the same word used for the woman's hemorrhage in Mark 5:25 (*rhysis haimatos*, "a flow of blood"), a deliberate verbal link that attentive Greek readers would have caught. Mark's story is, among other things, a Leviticus 15 story.

The LXX phrase *apo tēs sarcos autou* — "from his flesh" — in verse 2 mirrors the Hebrew *mibbesaro* with unusual literalism, preserving the physicality. The Greek text is not squeamish. The body is named. The flesh is named. The discharge is named. The LXX translators understood that there is no dignity in euphemism when the text is teaching about the conditions of the body's life before God.

The verb *aphorizō* — "to separate, set apart" — in verse 31 (LXX) is the root of *aphorismenos*, the term used in Galatians 1:15 for Paul being "set apart" (*aphōrismenos*) from birth. The word carries both a negative sense (exclusion, quarantine) and a positive sense (consecration, designation). In Leviticus 15:31, separating Israel from their impurities is an act of protection and care — the same word that will be used for prophetic commissioning. To be set apart from defilement is to be set apart *for* holiness. The direction of the movement matters: always toward the center, toward the dwelling, toward the God who chose to be in their midst.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 67r–67v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 101–102 (LXX).*
